evidence: Both files are pure contract-definition modules with only constant and descriptor values, with no direct imports or runtime side effects visible in these units.

In `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go`, each action contract (`ContractID`, `ActionNamespace`, `ActionToken`) is manually written as separate string literals instead of being derived through a shared constructor or compile-time binding.

The two files encode protocol surface contracts independently, which works today but increases coupling risk if protocol naming conventions evolve because consistency is enforced socially rather than structurally.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: No automatic validation/lint path is present in this layer to guarantee namespace-token-ID consistency, so cross-module contract consumers remain exposed to silent drift if IDs/tokens diverge.

evidence: goseg/main.go:562 hardcodes `StartupRuntime: startuporchestrator.StartupRuntime{}` when calling `startuporchestrator.Startup`, so callers of `runBootstrapSubsystems` cannot inject a composed startup runtime even when `runBootstrapSubsystems` is already parameterized with a `bootstrapRuntime`.
goseg/main.go:545-550 wraps callbacks into a `bootstrapRuntime` but only uses that runtime to seed `bootstrap()` and then always sends an empty startup runtime into the orchestrator.

README.md names a contract gate (`upload-contract-gate.yml`) and documents path-aware checks for contract surface changes, but the batch files themselves are declaration surfaces with no inline tests.
Contract contract metadata and bindings are validated through package tests outside the batch files, so confidence depends on consumers remembering to run those specific checks during contract edits.
The runtime docs list changed-files policy and deterministic-check expectations, but no explicit local enforcement appears embedded in these files themselves.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Increase confidence by making contract governance checks executable from these files (or nearby adjacent registry entry tests) so declaration edits cannot bypass the documented contract-test expectations.

evidence: Large, high-cluster test and runtime modules in scope still exhibit cohesion drift: `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` is 943 lines with multiple reset helpers, and `goseg/importer/importer_test.go` is 924 lines with repeated runtime fixture builders (`defaultImporterRuntime`, `resetUploadSessionsForTest`, `initRuntime`, `uploadRuntime`, `provisionRuntime`).
`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` defines many transition entrypoints and multiple step-builder helpers in one file, and mechanical signals flag mixed-responsibility clusters there.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` aggregates broad cross-cutting seam concerns (runtime, snapshots, urbit, image, volume, wireguard, minio, startup, health) in one contract surface with repeated callback-field patterns.

goseg/main.go ends the startup path with `os.Exit(1)` after logging `runBootstrapSubsystems` failure, which bypasses normal deferred cleanup/signaling flow despite returning a first-class error from `runBootstrapSubsystems`.

goseg/main.go uses a different end-state strategy for I/O/resource failures in backgrounded workflows compared to other files that capture close/teardown errors (e.g., `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`).
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Error handling is inconsistent: one path exits the process immediately while similar teardown/error points elsewhere preserve and propagate cleanup errors. Normalizing fatal startup/error paths with explicit shutdown/cleanup hooks would make behavior more predictable.

findings.summary: Contract ID strings are manually duplicated and can drift from namespace/token policy without structural enforcement.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` stores `ContractID`, `ActionNamespace`, and `ActionToken` as separate literals per action (e.g., open-endpoint upload variants).
`startram_contracts.go` separately defines error contract metadata and message literals, reinforcing the same pattern of manual contract catalog wiring.
There is no local helper/validator in these files to derive IDs and metadata from a single canonical contract specification.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared contract specification helper in `goseg/protocol/contracts` that requires namespace + token as inputs and derives `ContractID` centrally, and add an internal invariant test in the package that fails on malformed/mismatched contract strings before consumers import these contracts.

findings.summary: Bootstrap wiring drops an injectable startup runtime and always passes an empty runtime to the startup orchestrator.
evidence: `runBootstrapSubsystems` receives only `appStartupOptions` and a `bootstrapRuntime`, then builds `startuporchestrator.StartupOptions` with `StartupRuntime: startuporchestrator.StartupRuntime{}` (`goseg/main.go:554-563`).

The orchestrator path (`startuporchestrator.Bootstrap`) expects a runtime object for callback orchestration (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`), but this call-site ignores caller customization.
findings.suggestion: Thread an explicit `startuporchestrator.StartupRuntime` through `runBootstrapSubsystems` (and `main`) and pass it into `startuporchestrator.StartupOptions`, defaulting to `startuporchestrator.StartupRuntime{}` only at the outermost callsite. This keeps constructor seams intact and preserves test/embedding injection.

findings.summary: Test scaffolding patterns are duplicated across unrelated test suites instead of a shared harness.
evidence: `accesspoint_test.go` defines `resetParameterGlobalsForTest` and `resetRouterGlobalsForTest` with repeated `testseams.WithRestore` calls.

`importer_test.go` defines multiple runtime fixture builders and guards (`defaultImporterRuntime`, `initRuntime`, `uploadRuntime`, `provisionRuntime`) that replicate setup/restore mechanics also seen in other tests.
`main_test.go` includes repeated bootstrap runtime constructors (`c2cTestRuntime`, `bootstrapRuntimeWith`) plus callback assertions for equivalent orchestration behavior.
findings.suggestion: Create a test utility package in `goseg/internal/testutil` (or existing shared test seams package) that centralizes global restore helpers and runtime fixture constructors, then rewrite these tests to compose fixtures from shared helpers instead of duplicating the same restore/override patterns.

findings.summary: Ship workflow transitions mix too many transition concerns in one module.
evidence: `urbit_operations_runtime.go` includes many independent toggles (`toggleAlias`, `toggleNetwork`, `togglePower`, etc.) and matching `build*Transition` helpers in one large file.
Mechanical concern context already flags this file for responsibility_cohesion (`11 disconnected function clusters`), indicating the function grouping is not aligned by subsystem ownership.

Handlers continue to embed detailed transition workflows in this same domain area (`runTogglePowerTransition`, `runToggleNetworkTransition`), so orchestration, config persistence, and process control remain tightly coupled.
findings.suggestion: Split this module into focused files by concern (e.g., `runtime_toggle_power.go`, `runtime_toggle_network.go`, `runtime_config_handlers.go`) and register them through a small dispatch map to reduce semantic coupling and ease testing per transition domain.

findings.summary: Runtime seam bundle and operation structs mix unrelated domains into one interface surface.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` declares a broad matrix of unrelated ops structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeWireguardOps`, `RuntimeNetdataOps`, `RuntimeMinioOps`, `StartupLoadOps`, etc.) with shared seam plumbing.
The same file builds defaults for many independent concerns in a monolithic `buildRuntimeSeamBundle` flow.
Global review notes indicate runtime seam struct breadth and duplicated callback transport across import boundaries; this file is the likely concentration point.
findings.suggestion: Partition seam contracts into domain-specific seam interfaces/packages (runtime/container, urbit, persistence/snapshot, bootstrap/system, networking/security) and inject only the required interface into each caller instead of passing a broad bundle.

findings.summary: Configuration model, patch schema parsing, and apply semantics live in overly broad single modules.
evidence: `structs/configs.go` is 666 lines and defines multiple domain config sections, marshal/unmarshal behavior, section helpers, and many domain-specific mutators in one place.
`config_update_schema.go` is 440 lines with parser/validator/build-merge logic and many patch field abstractions in the same package surface.
This pairing forces tests and callers to touch a shared, high-entropy config module for many independent concerns rather than clear domain ownership.
findings.suggestion: Refactor config into domain modules (connectivity, runtime, startram, auth session, penpai) with explicit interfaces for parse/patch/apply per domain, then orchestrate composition at a thin root layer.

findings.summary: Boilerplate event and callback logic appears duplicated rather than extracted into a reusable adapter.
evidence: Packet-level concern log records boilerplate duplication across `subsystem_events.go` and `urbit_operations_runtime_handlers.go`.
Both areas implement near-identical patterns for callback-style orchestration plus transition/result handling without a shared adapter layer.
The resulting duplication makes updates to transition/error handling conventions harder to apply consistently.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a tiny handler utility for transition execution/error wrapping and event action dispatching, then replace duplicated local patterns in both files with that utility.

findings.summary: Top-level startup uses immediate process exit instead of propagating failure through shutdown/error pathways.
evidence: goseg/main.go logs the bootstrap error and then calls `os.Exit(1)` in `main` (around line 576).
goseg/main.go already has `main()`-scope context lifecycle setup (`signal.NotifyContext`, `defer cancel()`), but `os.Exit` skips deferred cleanup and can strand in-flight background resources.
findings.suggestion: Replace hard exit with a consistent shutdown path: return/route startup failure into a single terminator that first cancels context, stops background loops, closes resources, and then exits with status, so failures are handled uniformly across startup failure scenarios.

findings.summary: Resource close errors in listener cleanup are silently dropped instead of being preserved or surfaced.
evidence: goseg/leak/broadcast.go cleanup block calls `conn.Close()` directly without handling the returned error.
goseg/importer/importer.go and goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go use close/error-join helpers to retain and surface close failures when closing I/O resources.
findings.suggestion: Wrap `conn.Close()` with the same close-error pattern used elsewhere (e.g., `resource.JoinCloseError`) or log/propagate closure failure explicitly, so leak listener shutdown failures are not silently hidden.

findings.summary: Accesspoint router setup can panic on valid but non-IPv4 IP inputs due to brittle string slicing assumptions.
evidence: `checkParameters` in accesspoint config allows generic `net.ParseIP` values, including formats outside dotted-quad.
`startRouterWithRuntime` path relies on `ipParts := rt.IP[:strings.LastIndex(rt.IP, ".")]` for DNS range generation, which is unsafe when `rt.IP` is not IPv4 and `LastIndex` is -1.
This fault appears in the startup path before runtime recovery and can terminate startup on user-supplied/derived IP variants.
findings.suggestion: Normalize and validate `IP` as IPv4 early (`To4` + explicit CIDR/format checks), then safely handle invalid/non-IPv4 values with explicit error returns; keep validation in the same boundary as the code that later slices components.

findings.summary: Boolean patch fields are treated as tri-state-like values in the schema but merge helpers can ignore intentional false updates.
evidence: `allConfPatchFields` registers many boolean config fields through helpers that do not preserve false as an explicit update in merge semantics.
Callers consuming `config patch` updates interpret those fields as authoritative, so false cannot be reliably persisted for several toggles.
findings.suggestion: Replace boolean merge behavior with explicit tri-state handling (e.g., pointer/optional bool in patch input) or schema tags that distinguish omission from false, then align all executors to apply the same rule.

findings.summary: Transition event bus drops are possible under load and are not surfaced to orchestration callers, creating hidden control-plane divergence.
evidence: Event publishing uses bounded transport and returns `ErrTransitionBusFull` only when channel capacity is exceeded.
In orchestration paths, transition publication is invoked without mandatory handling of this error path, so saturation produces silent event loss.
This breaks eventual consistency assumptions between transition producers and lifecycle consumers.
findings.suggestion: Promote queue overflow from best-effort to policy-driven behavior: propagate errors, add metrics/tracing for overflow, and define per-bus retry/circuit policy so callers cannot proceed as if delivery were guaranteed.

findings.summary: High-risk production paths lack focused tests for malformed inputs and saturation/fallback behavior.
evidence: Validation logic in `accesspoint` and merge semantics in `config` directly encode safety/consistency behavior but are not covered by visible unit tests in the reviewed batch.
Transition bus overflow and dropped publication behavior is not guarded by tests simulating full-channel/backpressure scenarios.
findings.suggestion: Add table-driven unit tests for IP and patch-schema validation, plus event-bus concurrency tests that assert publish failures are surfaced and recovered/propagated according to contract.
